---
title: "Case Study: Cedar Lake Roving Creel Survey"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(tidycreel)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(lubridate)
library(forcats)
library(patchwork)
library(here)

cedar_schedule   <- readRDS(here("data/cedar_schedule.rds"))
cedar_counts     <- readRDS(here("data/cedar_counts.rds"))
cedar_interviews <- readRDS(here("data/cedar_interviews.rds"))
cedar_catch      <- readRDS(here("data/cedar_catch.rds"))

# Shift hours: both periods 6 hours (6 amâ€“noon and noonâ€“6 pm)
shift_hrs <- c("1" = 6L, "2" = 6L)

# Counts: pre-compute angler_hours, retain only count variable (Ch12 pattern)
cedar_counts_effort <- cedar_counts |>
  mutate(
    period       = as.character(period),
    angler_hours = (bank_anglers + boat_anglers) * shift_hrs[period]
  ) |>
  select(reservoir, date, day_type, period, section, count_time, angler_hours)

# Catch wide: harvested + released columns required by add_interviews()
catch_wide <- cedar_catch |>
  group_by(interview_id, catch_type) |>
  summarise(n = sum(n_fish), .groups = "drop") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = catch_type, values_from = n, values_fill = 0L) |>
  mutate(total_catch = harvested + released)

cedar_int2 <- cedar_interviews |>
  mutate(period = as.character(period)) |>
  left_join(catch_wide, by = "interview_id") |>
  mutate(across(c(harvested, released, total_catch),
                ~ if_else(is.na(.), 0L, as.integer(.))))

# Build design object
suppressMessages({
  cedar_design <- creel_design(
    calendar = cedar_schedule,
    date     = "date",
    strata   = "day_type"
  ) |>
    add_counts(cedar_counts_effort, count_time_col = "count_time") |>
    add_interviews(
      interviews     = cedar_int2,
      catch          = "total_catch",
      harvest        = "harvested",
      effort         = "hours_fished",
      trip_duration  = "hours_fished",
      trip_status    = "trip_status",
      n_anglers      = "n_anglers",
      angler_type    = "angler_type",
      angler_method  = "angler_method",
      interview_type = "roving"
    ) |>
    add_catch(
      data          = cedar_catch,
      catch_uid     = "interview_id",
      interview_uid = "interview_id",
      species       = "species",
      count         = "n_fish",
      catch_type    = "catch_type"
    )
})

# Pre-compute all season-level estimates for inline R
eff_tot   <- estimate_effort(cedar_design, target = "stratum_total")
eff_strat <- estimate_effort(cedar_design, by = day_type, target = "stratum_total")
harv_tot  <- estimate_total_harvest(cedar_design, target = "stratum_total")
harv_strat <- estimate_total_harvest(cedar_design, by = day_type, target = "stratum_total")
cat_tot   <- estimate_total_catch(cedar_design, target = "stratum_total")
rel_tot   <- estimate_total_release(cedar_design, target = "stratum_total")
cpue_all  <- estimate_catch_rate(cedar_design)
cpue_dt   <- estimate_catch_rate(cedar_design, by = day_type)
cpue_at   <- estimate_catch_rate(cedar_design, by = angler_type)
hpue_all  <- estimate_harvest_rate(cedar_design)

# Formatting helpers
fmt_comma <- function(x) formatC(round(x), format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")
fmt_rate  <- function(x) sprintf("%.2f", x)
fmt_pct   <- function(x) sprintf("%.1f%%", x)
fmt_cv    <- function(est) fmt_pct(100 * est$se / est$estimate)
```

## Setting and design overview

Harlan Reservoir anchors Part IV: a large impoundment sampled by access-point design, dominated by boat anglers, and operated over a single calendar year at high daily sampling intensity. Cedar Lake represents a complementary setting â€” a small urban reservoir managed primarily for walk-in anglers who do not require a boat ramp.

Cedar Lake was surveyed under a roving creel design. A single clerk drove the lake perimeter twice per sampling period, recording instantaneous counts of all visible anglers and stopping to interview parties encountered mid-trip. Unlike an access-point survey, roving contacts happen while anglers are still fishing: the clerk records fish caught so far and time elapsed so far, then continues the circuit. The result is a high fraction of incomplete-trip interviews and a catch-rate estimator that must account for the truncated trip record.

The fishery targets channel catfish, bluegill, and white crappie across a seven-month open-water season, April through October 2023. Weekend pressure peaks in June and July, while shoulder months in April and October see reduced effort driven by cold weather and occasional survey-day cancellations due to rain or dangerous conditions.

This chapter walks the full analysis pipeline â€” frame to final report â€” using Cedar Lake as the vehicle.

## Data overview

Four linked tables describe the 2023 Cedar Lake survey:

```{r}
#| label: cedar-summary
n_days   <- nrow(cedar_schedule)
n_samp   <- sum(cedar_schedule$sampled)
n_int    <- nrow(cedar_interviews)
n_comp   <- sum(cedar_interviews$trip_status == "complete")
n_incomp <- sum(cedar_interviews$trip_status == "incomplete")
pct_bank <- 100 * mean(cedar_interviews$angler_type == "bank")

cat(
  "Survey period:   ", format(min(cedar_schedule$date), "%B %d"), "â€“",
  format(max(cedar_schedule$date), "%B %d, %Y"), "\n",
  "Total days:      ", n_days, "\n",
  "Sampled days:    ", n_samp, sprintf("(%.0f%%)\n", 100 * n_samp / n_days),
  "Interviews:      ", n_int,
  sprintf("(%d complete, %d incomplete)\n", n_comp, n_incomp),
  "Bank anglers:    ", sprintf("%.0f%% of interviews\n", pct_bank),
  sep = ""
)
```

The `r fmt_pct(100 * n_incomp / n_int)` incomplete-trip rate reflects the roving design: every interview happens mid-trip, capturing only elapsed time and fish so far. Complete trips â€” parties who happened to be finishing when the clerk passed â€” account for `r fmt_pct(100 * n_comp / n_int)` of contacts. Total-catch estimation requires the full trip record and uses only this subset; the mean-of-ratios CPUE estimator treats each mid-trip contact as an independent instantaneous catch-rate observation and uses every interview regardless of completion status.

### Seasonal survey coverage

Sampling intensity followed a proportional design: more survey days were allocated to summer months when angler pressure peaks. Eighteen survey days were cancelled or produced zero-angler counts due to rain, cold fronts, or high winds.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cedar-season
#| fig-cap: "Monthly sampling effort and mean instantaneous angler count at Cedar Lake, 2023. Left: number of sampled days per month by stratum. Right: mean total anglers per count by month. Zero-count days from adverse weather appear in both panels."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 3.5

monthly_days <- cedar_schedule |>
  filter(sampled) |>
  mutate(month = factor(format(date, "%b"), levels = month.abb)) |>
  count(month, day_type)

monthly_counts <- cedar_counts |>
  mutate(
    month = factor(format(date, "%b"), levels = month.abb),
    total = bank_anglers + boat_anglers
  ) |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(mean_count = mean(total), .groups = "drop")

p1 <- ggplot(monthly_days, aes(month, n, fill = day_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(weekday = "#6baed6", weekend = "#2171b5"),
                    name = "Day type") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Sampled days") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p2 <- ggplot(monthly_counts, aes(month, mean_count)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#74c476") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean anglers per count") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p1 + p2
```

The seasonal arc is clear: mean instantaneous counts rise from `r round(monthly_counts$mean_count[monthly_counts$month == "Apr"], 1)` anglers per count in April to `r round(monthly_counts$mean_count[monthly_counts$month == "Aug"], 1)` in August before falling through October. Zero or near-zero count days are visible in April and October, reflecting cancelled surveys during cold or high-wind conditions.

### Angler composition across the season

Bank anglers dominate the Cedar Lake count record, but their fraction varies slightly by month as the small boat contingent fluctuates.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cedar-bankboat
#| fig-cap: "Monthly angler composition at Cedar Lake, 2023, from instantaneous count records. Bank anglers (dark) dominate throughout the season; the boat fraction increases slightly in midsummer. Zero-count days from adverse weather reduce monthly totals without changing the proportion."
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 3.2

cedar_counts |>
  mutate(month = factor(format(date, "%b"), levels = month.abb)) |>
  group_by(month) |>
  summarise(
    bank = sum(bank_anglers),
    boat = sum(boat_anglers),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(c(bank, boat), names_to = "type", values_to = "count") |>
  ggplot(aes(month, count, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(position = "fill") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(bank = "#74c476", boat = "#2171b5"),
                    name = "Angler type") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Fraction of counted anglers",
       title = "Monthly bank vs. boat composition") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Bank anglers account for `r fmt_pct(100 * sum(cedar_counts$bank_anglers) / sum(cedar_counts$bank_anglers + cedar_counts$boat_anglers))` of all counted anglers across the season. This compositional stability simplifies interpretation: the season-level CPUE by angler type is not confounded by a seasonal shift in who is on the water.

### Incomplete trips in a roving design

```{r}
#| label: cedar-trip-summary
summarize_trips(cedar_design)
```

`summarize_trips()` reports median and mean trip duration separately for complete and incomplete trips. At Cedar Lake, the median complete trip lasts considerably longer than the median time-fished reported for incomplete contacts â€” an expected consequence of length-biased sampling. Anglers encountered mid-circuit have already been fishing for some time (so they are more likely to have started early or plan a long trip), which means the elapsed-time record understates the eventual full-trip duration.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-incomplete-check
validate_incomplete_trips(
  cedar_design,
  catch   = harvested,
  effort  = hours_fished,
  truncate_at = 0.5
)
```

`validate_incomplete_trips()` runs a two-one-sided t-test (TOST) equivalence test comparing harvest-per-hour between incomplete and complete trips. A non-significant result means the data are consistent with rate equivalence â€” that incomplete trips are not selectively capturing faster or slower anglers. Equivalence within Â± 0.5 fish/hr is the default tolerance. If the test rejects equivalence, the analyst should investigate whether a particular time window (early morning, pre-storm) drives a systematic bias in which anglers are contacted mid-trip.

## Building the design object

The Cedar Lake pipeline follows the same four-step assembly used throughout Part III and Part IV. Three features differ from the Harlan build:

1. **Both periods have equal shift length.** Harlan's morning shift runs 8 hours; Cedar Lake uses 6 hours for each period (6 amâ€“noon, noonâ€“6 pm). The shift-hours vector reflects that equality.
2. **Counts carry only `angler_hours`.** Bank and boat counts are summed and multiplied by shift length before calling `add_counts()`. The component columns are dropped so `tidycreel` selects only the target variable.
3. **Two catch columns required.** `estimate_total_harvest()` requires a dedicated harvest column passed via the `harvest` argument. Total catch (harvested + released) is a separate column passed via `catch`.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-counts-prep
# Pre-compute angler_hours; drop component columns
cedar_counts_effort |>
  filter(date == as.Date("2023-07-08")) |>
  select(date, day_type, period, count_time, angler_hours)
```

```{r}
#| label: cedar-int-prep
# Interview table with both catch columns
cedar_int2 |>
  select(interview_id, date, trip_status, angler_type, hours_fished,
         harvested, released, total_catch) |>
  slice(1:6)
```

The split between `harvested` and `total_catch` is what allows the estimation layer to produce HPUE (harvest per unit effort) and CPUE (catch per unit effort) as distinct quantities from the same interview record.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-design-build
#| eval: false
cedar_design <- creel_design(
  calendar = cedar_schedule,
  date     = "date",
  strata   = "day_type"
) |>
  add_counts(cedar_counts_effort, count_time_col = "count_time") |>
  add_interviews(
    interviews     = cedar_int2,
    catch          = "total_catch",
    harvest        = "harvested",
    effort         = "hours_fished",
    trip_duration  = "hours_fished",
    trip_status    = "trip_status",
    n_anglers      = "n_anglers",
    angler_type    = "angler_type",
    angler_method  = "angler_method",
    interview_type = "roving"
  ) |>
  add_catch(
    data          = cedar_catch,
    catch_uid     = "interview_id",
    interview_uid = "interview_id",
    species       = "species",
    count         = "n_fish",
    catch_type    = "catch_type"
  )
```

The design object stores the frame, counts, interviews, and catch in a single structure. All estimation functions read from this object rather than from the raw tables, ensuring consistent stratum labels, consistent day-type assignments, and a reproducible audit trail.

## Effort estimation

Total angler-hours is estimated from instantaneous count data. Each sampled day contributes two count replicates per period; `add_counts()` averages replicates within a period before computing day-period effort as `mean_count Ă— shift_hours`. The design then expands sampled-day effort to the full survey period using the ratio of total days to sampled days within each stratum.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-effort
eff_tot$estimates
```

Cedar Lake logged an estimated `r fmt_comma(eff_tot$estimates$estimate)` angler-hours during the 2023 open-water season (95% CI: `r fmt_comma(eff_tot$estimates$ci_lower)`â€“`r fmt_comma(eff_tot$estimates$ci_upper)`). Weekend and weekday effort are nearly balanced, consistent with an urban fishery that draws regular weekday use from retirees and shift workers in addition to weekend family outings.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-effort-strat
eff_strat$estimates
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-cedar-effort
#| fig-cap: "Season-total effort estimates by day-type stratum, Cedar Lake 2023. Points are ratio-of-means estimates; bars are 95% confidence intervals."
#| fig-width: 5.5
#| fig-height: 2.8

eff_strat$estimates |>
  ggplot(aes(estimate, fct_rev(day_type))) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = ci_lower, xmax = ci_upper),
                 height = 0.15, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 3, shape = 21, fill = "#6baed6") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Estimated effort (angler-hours)", y = NULL,
       title = "Season effort by day type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

Both strata carry overlapping confidence intervals. A formal two-group comparison is not the goal here â€” this is a reporting display, not a hypothesis test. The overlap tells managers that weekday and weekend programs would both see meaningful angler engagement.

The overall effort CV is `r fmt_pct(100 * eff_tot$estimates$se / eff_tot$estimates$estimate)`, which falls within the 10â€“20% management-grade range for a stratified open-water creel survey. Between-day variance dominates: `r fmt_pct(100 * eff_tot$estimates$se_between / eff_tot$estimates$se)` of the standard error comes from day-to-day variability in count totals rather than within-day count replication.

## Catch, harvest, and release

Cedar Lake targets three species with different management priorities: channel catfish (the primary trophy target, regulated by a daily bag limit), bluegill (abundant panfish, essentially unrestricted harvest), and white crappie (intermediate in both abundance and regulations).

### Species catch composition

```{r}
#| label: fig-cedar-species
#| fig-cap: "Total fish recorded in Cedar Lake interviews by species and catch type. Bars show absolute counts from the interview catch table before expansion; proportions within species reflect true harvest and release fractions."
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 3.2

cedar_catch |>
  group_by(species, catch_type) |>
  summarise(total = sum(n_fish), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(species = fct_reorder(species, total, .fun = sum)) |>
  ggplot(aes(total, species, fill = catch_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(harvested = "#fd8d3c", released = "#74c476"),
                    name = "Catch type") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(x = "Fish recorded in interviews", y = NULL,
       title = "Interview catch composition by species") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Bluegill dominates raw catch counts â€” consistent with panfish abundance in a shallow, vegetated urban reservoir. Channel catfish show a markedly higher harvest fraction than the other two species, reflecting angler behavior: catfish are kept at high rates while bluegill and crappie see more selective harvest.

### Season-level estimates

```{r}
#| label: cedar-harvest
harv_tot$estimates
cat_tot$estimates
rel_tot$estimates
```

The season totals reveal asymmetry in estimation precision. Total harvest uses the complete interview set (`n` = `r harv_tot$estimates$n`) via the ratio-of-means estimator, while total catch uses only the `r cat_tot$estimates$n` completed trips. `estimate_total_release()` returns an estimate from the full interview set (`n` = `r rel_tot$estimates$n`). This means harvest and release estimates draw on different subsets of the data, and their sum will not exactly equal the total-catch estimate. Chapter 13 discusses this asymmetry in detail; the short version is that release information is available for incomplete trips (fish visible in livewell or on stringer mid-trip), while total catch requires the completed trip record.

Harvest by stratum shows modest weekdayâ€“weekend differences, both within the confidence intervals of the season total:

```{r}
#| label: cedar-harv-strat
harv_strat$estimates
```

Season total harvest: `r fmt_comma(harv_tot$estimates$estimate)` fish (95% CI: `r fmt_comma(harv_tot$estimates$ci_lower)`â€“`r fmt_comma(harv_tot$estimates$ci_upper)`). Season total catch: `r fmt_comma(cat_tot$estimates$estimate)` fish.

## CPUE and harvest rate

Roving designs use the mean-of-ratios (MOR) estimator for catch rate, not the ratio-of-means (ROM) used in access-point surveys. Each mid-trip contact supplies an independent observation of instantaneous catch rate â€” fish caught so far divided by time fished so far â€” a valid rate measurement regardless of whether the trip ultimately completes. With `r fmt_pct(100 * n_incomp / n_int)` incomplete contacts at Cedar Lake, the MOR estimator retains `r cpue_all$estimates$n` of `r n_int` interviews (eight very short contacts under 0.5 hours are excluded as unreliable rate observations). Chapter 14 discusses the ROM vs. MOR distinction and when each is appropriate.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-cpue-all
cpue_all$estimates
hpue_all$estimates
```

Overall CPUE is `r fmt_rate(cpue_all$estimates$estimate)` fish per angler-hour (95% CI: `r fmt_rate(cpue_all$estimates$ci_lower)`â€“`r fmt_rate(cpue_all$estimates$ci_upper)`). The harvest rate is `r fmt_rate(hpue_all$estimates$estimate)` fish per angler-hour, meaning anglers kept `r fmt_pct(100 * hpue_all$estimates$estimate / cpue_all$estimates$estimate)` of all fish caught.

### CPUE by day type

```{r}
#| label: cedar-cpue-daytype
cpue_dt$estimates
```

Weekday and weekend CPUE are nearly identical â€” `r fmt_rate(cpue_dt$estimates$estimate[cpue_dt$estimates$day_type == "weekday"])` and `r fmt_rate(cpue_dt$estimates$estimate[cpue_dt$estimates$day_type == "weekend"])` fish per angler-hour respectively. Overlapping confidence intervals confirm that there is no detectable difference in catch success between the two strata at this sample size.

### CPUE by angler type

Bank and boat anglers differ in access, target species, and technique. Bank anglers at Cedar Lake tend to use bait (worms, prepared dough) fished near vegetated structure, targeting bluegill and catfish; the small boat fleet focuses more on open-water crappie. The split in CPUE reflects those behavioral differences.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cedar-cpue-type
#| fig-cap: "Catch per angler-hour by angler type at Cedar Lake, 2023. Points are mean-of-ratios estimates using all interviewed trips; bars are 95% confidence intervals. Boat stratum sample size is small (n < 50 contacts); variance estimate is less stable."
#| fig-width: 5.5
#| fig-height: 2.8

cpue_at$estimates |>
  ggplot(aes(estimate, fct_rev(angler_type))) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = ci_lower, xmax = ci_upper),
                 height = 0.15, linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(shape = angler_type), size = 3, fill = "#6baed6") +
  scale_shape_manual(values = c(bank = 21, boat = 24), guide = "none") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = cpue_all$estimates$estimate, linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.5, color = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = cpue_all$estimates$estimate + 0.03,
           y = Inf, vjust = 1.5, hjust = 0, size = 3,
           label = sprintf("Overall: %.2f", cpue_all$estimates$estimate)) +
  labs(x = "Catch per angler-hour", y = NULL,
       title = "CPUE by angler type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)
```

Bank anglers catch `r fmt_rate(cpue_at$estimates$estimate[cpue_at$estimates$angler_type == "bank"])` fish per angler-hour; boat anglers catch `r fmt_rate(cpue_at$estimates$estimate[cpue_at$estimates$angler_type == "boat"])` fish per angler-hour. The boat sample is small (n = `r cpue_at$estimates$n[cpue_at$estimates$angler_type == "boat"]` trips), so the wider confidence interval should be interpreted cautiously. At current sample sizes, a bank versus boat contrast is suggestive but not conclusive.

## Precision and diagnostics

A precision profile summarizes CV across all season-level estimates. The goal is to identify which quantities are estimated confidently enough for management use and which carry enough uncertainty to warrant a caveated interpretation.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-cv-summary
cv_eff   <- 100 * eff_tot$estimates$se   / eff_tot$estimates$estimate
cv_harv  <- 100 * harv_tot$estimates$se  / harv_tot$estimates$estimate
cv_catch <- 100 * cat_tot$estimates$se   / cat_tot$estimates$estimate
cv_cpue  <- 100 * cpue_all$estimates$se  / cpue_all$estimates$estimate
cv_hpue  <- 100 * hpue_all$estimates$se  / hpue_all$estimates$estimate

cv_tbl <- tibble::tibble(
  quantity = c("Effort", "Total harvest", "Total catch", "CPUE", "HPUE"),
  category = c("effort", "total", "total", "rate", "rate"),
  cv = c(cv_eff, cv_harv, cv_catch, cv_cpue, cv_hpue)
)
cv_tbl
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-cedar-precision
#| fig-cap: "Coefficient of variation (%) for season-level estimates, Cedar Lake 2023. Dashed line at 20% is a common management-grade threshold for acceptable precision."
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 3.2

cv_tbl |>
  mutate(quantity = fct_reorder(quantity, cv)) |>
  ggplot(aes(cv, quantity, fill = category)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 20, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.6, color = "grey30") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(effort = "#6baed6", total = "#fd8d3c", rate = "#74c476"),
    name = "Estimate type"
  ) +
  labs(x = "CV (%)", y = NULL, title = "Precision profile") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Effort CV is `r fmt_pct(cv_eff)` â€” the most precisely estimated quantity, because it draws on the full set of `r n_samp` sampled days. Total harvest CV is `r fmt_pct(cv_harv)`, within the acceptable range. Total catch CV is `r fmt_pct(cv_catch)`, highest of the group because only the `r n_comp` completed trips contribute to that estimator. CPUE and HPUE have the smallest CVs â€” `r fmt_pct(cv_cpue)` and `r fmt_pct(cv_hpue)` respectively â€” reflecting both the large MOR sample (`r cpue_all$estimates$n` contacts) and the correlated-quotient property of rate estimators, which partially cancels between-day variance in effort and catch.

Quantities above 20% CV deserve a caveated interpretation in the final report. For Cedar Lake 2023, all season-level totals and rates fall within the management-grade range. The boat-angler CPUE sub-group, while not shown in this summary, carries a much larger CV and should not be reported as a standalone number without a prominent uncertainty statement.

## Final report assembly

All seven estimates are assembled into a single reporting tibble, formatted for a non-technical audience. The pattern mirrors Chapter 16: bind rows, add formatted strings, compute CV column.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-report-assembly
report_summary <- bind_rows(
  eff_tot$estimates   |> mutate(quantity = "Effort",         unit = "angler-hours"),
  harv_tot$estimates  |> mutate(quantity = "Total harvest",  unit = "fish"),
  cat_tot$estimates   |> mutate(quantity = "Total catch",    unit = "fish"),
  rel_tot$estimates   |> mutate(quantity = "Total release",  unit = "fish"),
  cpue_all$estimates  |> mutate(quantity = "CPUE",           unit = "fish/hr"),
  hpue_all$estimates  |> mutate(quantity = "HPUE",           unit = "fish/hr")
) |>
  select(quantity, unit, estimate, se, ci_lower, ci_upper)

report_summary
```

```{r}
#| label: cedar-report-cv
report_table <- report_summary |>
  mutate(
    cv = case_when(
      as.character(unit) %in% c("fish/hr") ~ fmt_pct(100 * se / estimate),
      TRUE ~ fmt_pct(100 * se / estimate)
    ),
    estimate_fmt = if_else(
      unit %in% c("fish/hr"),
      fmt_rate(estimate),
      fmt_comma(estimate)
    ),
    ci_fmt = paste0(
      if_else(unit %in% c("fish/hr"), fmt_rate(ci_lower), fmt_comma(ci_lower)),
      "â€“",
      if_else(unit %in% c("fish/hr"), fmt_rate(ci_upper), fmt_comma(ci_upper))
    )
  ) |>
  select(Quantity = quantity, Unit = unit,
         Estimate = estimate_fmt, `95% CI` = ci_fmt, CV = cv)

knitr::kable(report_table, align = "llrrr",
             caption = "Cedar Lake 2023 creel survey season estimates.")
```

### Inline narrative

A manager's summary for Cedar Lake 2023 draws directly from the computed estimates:

::: {.callout-note title="Example: manager one-page summary (excerpt)"}
Cedar Lake anglers logged an estimated `r fmt_comma(eff_tot$estimates$estimate)` angler-hours during the 2023 open-water season (April 1â€“October 31), with weekday effort (`r fmt_comma(eff_strat$estimates$estimate[eff_strat$estimates$day_type == "weekday"])` angler-hours) and weekend effort (`r fmt_comma(eff_strat$estimates$estimate[eff_strat$estimates$day_type == "weekend"])` angler-hours) nearly equal. Total harvest across all species was `r fmt_comma(harv_tot$estimates$estimate)` fish (95% CI: `r fmt_comma(harv_tot$estimates$ci_lower)`â€“`r fmt_comma(harv_tot$estimates$ci_upper)` fish). Anglers caught `r fmt_rate(cpue_all$estimates$estimate)` fish per angler-hour and retained `r fmt_rate(hpue_all$estimates$estimate)` fish per angler-hour. These estimates are based on `r n_samp` sampled survey days and `r n_int` angler contacts (`r n_comp` completed trips).
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Example: technical report footnote"}
Effort uses ratio-of-means with day-type stratification from instantaneous count data. CPUE and HPUE use the mean-of-ratios estimator with all `r cpue_all$estimates$n` contacts (8 trips under 0.5 hours excluded); total catch uses only the `r n_comp` completed trips (`r fmt_pct(100 * n_comp / n_int)` of all contacts); total harvest and release use the full interview set (n = `r n_int`). Confidence intervals are 95% normal-approximation intervals from the design-based variance estimator. Domain: Cedar Lake public-access shoreline, daylight hours, April 1â€“October 31, 2023.
:::

### Species-level CPUE

Cedar Lake targets three species with different catchability profiles.
`estimate_catch_rate(by = species)` returns design-based estimates with
stratification weights and confidence intervals for each:

```{r}
#| label: cedar-species-cpue
estimate_catch_rate(cedar_design, by = species)
```

Bluegill accounts for the highest CPUE, consistent with shallow vegetated
habitat where panfish densities are high and contacts are frequent.
Channel catfish CPUE is lower on a per-angler-hour basis but represents a
disproportionate share of harvest by weight â€” catfish are kept at high rates.
White crappie CPUE is lowest overall, reflecting both lower abundance and
seasonal availability; crappie concentrate in predictable locations in spring
and fall but disperse to deeper structure in summer.

## Cedar Lake versus Harlan: a design contrast

Cedar Lake and Harlan share the same estimation engine â€” `creel_design`, `add_counts`, `add_interviews`, `add_catch`, `estimate_effort`, `estimate_total_harvest`, `estimate_catch_rate` â€” but the two surveys differ in nearly every operational detail. Comparing them reveals which parts of the workflow are universal and which require lake-specific decisions.

```{r}
#| label: cedar-harlan-compare
compare_tbl <- tibble::tribble(
  ~Feature,                   ~`Harlan Reservoir`,        ~`Cedar Lake`,
  "Survey period",            "Full calendar year",        "Aprilâ€“October",
  "Design type",              "Access-point",              "Roving circuit",
  "Shift hours",              "8 hr / 6 hr (P1/P2)",       "6 hr / 6 hr (equal)",
  "Angler composition",       "~70% boat",                 "~88% bank",
  "Interview completion",     "~65% complete",             "~29% complete",
  "Primary species",          "White bass, walleye",       "Bluegill, catfish, crappie",
  "Season effort",            "~33,000 angler-hours",
    sprintf("~%s angler-hours", fmt_comma(eff_tot$estimates$estimate)),
  "CPUE",                     "1.51 fish/hr",
    sprintf("%s fish/hr", fmt_rate(cpue_all$estimates$estimate)),
  "Effort CV",                "~12%",                      fmt_pct(cv_eff),
  "Harvest CV",               "~27%",                      fmt_pct(cv_harv)
)

knitr::kable(compare_tbl, align = "lll",
             caption = "Design and estimate comparison between Harlan Reservoir and Cedar Lake.")
```

The most consequential difference is the incomplete-trip rate. Harlan was operated as an access-point survey, so most interviews captured completed trips; Cedar Lake's roving protocol contacts parties mid-trip, meaning `r fmt_pct(100 * n_incomp / n_int)` of all records are incomplete. The effect on total-count estimation is asymmetric: total-catch requires the complete trip record (n = `r n_comp`), while harvest and release totals draw on the full interview pool. Catch rates are less affected â€” the MOR estimator uses every mid-trip contact as an instantaneous rate observation, so CPUE precision is not penalized by the high incomplete-trip rate. The binding precision constraint for roving surveys is total catch, not CPUE; managers who need tighter total-catch confidence intervals must increase sampled days or supplement with access-point exits.

A second consequence of the roving design is the lower absolute effort estimate. Cedar Lake is a much smaller water body than Harlan â€” fewer anglers, shorter average trips, and a seasonal window limited to seven months. The per-angler-hour CPUE is actually higher at Cedar Lake (`r fmt_rate(cpue_all$estimates$estimate)` vs 1.51 fish/hr), reflecting the dense panfish community. Scale differences like this make across-lake comparisons misleading unless normalized per unit area or per unit effort.

## Reproducibility note

The Cedar Lake estimates in this chapter will reproduce exactly given the same seed, data files, and package version:

```{r}
#| label: cedar-session
cat("tidycreel:", as.character(packageVersion("tidycreel")), "\n")
cat("R:         ", R.version$version.string, "\n")
cat("Data seed: 2023 (set in data/cedar_*.rds generation)\n")
```

Production use should version-control the four `.rds` files alongside the analysis script. Saving the design object avoids rerunning the pipeline for downstream reporting:

```{r}
#| label: cedar-save-design
#| eval: false
saveRDS(cedar_design, here("data/cedar_design_2023.rds"))
```

Loading the saved design in a future session restores the full stratum structure, counts, and interview data in a single call â€” identical to rebuilding the design from raw tables, but without the preparation steps.
